docs.title: 4D-Bio
docs.text:
Welcome to 4D-Bio!

4D-Bio is the first clear roadmap to AI-fluency in Bioinformatics. Inspired by Anthropic’s 4D Framework for AI fluency and the ISCB Competency Framework, this professional practice model maps classic bioinformatics skills across the four AI-fluency competencies of Anthropic's framework (Delegation, Description, Discernment, and Dilligence) and contextualizes them into the professional practice of AI-augmented Bioinformatics.

This open-source repository serves as a living resource for the community to drive the responsible integration of generative AI into our professional activities as the field evolves. It is a call to the community of bioinformatics professionals to come together to drive the transformation of our profession. As domain experts, it is on us to determine how generative AI gets incorporated into our daily work, and to show decision-makers why our expertise remains at the cornerstone of every major investment in life and health sciences.

docs.title: Our Mission
docs.text: To foster an inclusive and accountable professional community of bioinformaticians commited to upholding scientific rigor and ensuring reproducibility of computational life sciences research in the age of AI.

docs.title: Our Purpose
docs.text:
The main purpose of this repository is to be a town square for the community to shape the guidelines for the professional practice of bioinformatics in the age of agentic AI.

AI technology is moving faster than the rate of scientific publishing. The pace of new information is simply too overwhelming for any one of us to keep up with alone. By creating this repo, my hope is that anyone with something interesting to share can do so, regardless of their resources. We amplify the impact of regional efforts by capturing discussions, resources, and lessons learned in a central point whithout borders, politics, organizational interests.

docs.title: How to use this site
docs.text:
This site is a living document. You can navigate through the practice model using the sidebar. If you have questions or ideas, we invite you to participate in our community discussions at the bottom of each page or contribute directly to this text by clicking the edit button at the top right.

The easiest and most helpful thing you can do is share your thoughts in the community forum section of our Discussions page:

  • 📖 AI Errors Archive: Document examples of cases where AI failed and how you caught the mistake.
  • 🔮 Future of Bioinformatics: Share your view on what you want and don't want the future of AI-augmented bioinformatics to be like.
  • 🧬 Model Feedback: Share examples of how the professional practice model helped you build or improve something, like a new workshop or a course curriculum.

Other was to contribute:

  • See a typo? Click the pencil icon in the top-right corner of any page to propose a fix.
  • Want to add to the model? Follow our Contributing Guide.
  • Want to join the moderators/mainteiners team? Keep an eye out for the application form!

For more details see the Contributor Guide.

This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International License.

This resource is based on the AI Fluency Framework developed by Prof. Rick Dakan, Prof. Joseph Feller, and Anthropic, used under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. To learn more about the framework and it's associated educational resources see Anthropic's AI Fluency: Framework & Foundations site.

docs.title: Our Pledge
docs.text: We as members, contributors, and leaders pledge to make participation in our community a harassment-free experience for everyone, regardless of age, body size, disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity and expression, level of experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, religion, or sexual identity and orientation.

docs.title: Our Standards
docs.text: Examples of behavior that contributes to a positive environment include: - Using welcoming and inclusive language. - Being respectful of differing viewpoints and experiences. - Gracefully accepting constructive criticism. - Focusing on what is best for the community. - Showing empathy towards other community members.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
This Code of Conduct applies both within project spaces and in public spaces when an individual is representing the project or its community.

(For a full, formal version of this text, visit: https://www.contributor-covenant.org/version/2/1/code_of_conduct/)

docs.title: Contributing to this Project
docs.text:
We love contributions! Whether you have a small correction or a massive new idea, here is how you can help:

  • Community Discussions: Join the conversation on our Discussions page.
  • Reporting Issues: Did you find something wrong? Open an Issue and let us know.
  • Proposing Edits: Click the "Edit" pencil icon on any page. This will take you to GitHub's web editor. Once you save, you'll be creating a "Pull Request."

docs.title: How to contribute to the discussions
docs.text:
The Discussions Discussions page has two sections: the community forum, and the general purpose section. The primary goal of the community forum is to become a shared knowledgebase to increase the impact of informal conversations and individual actions. You'd be suprised of how much you can help others simply by sharing your experience!

  • 📖 AI Errors Archive: Document examples of cases where AI failed and how you caught the mistake.
  • 🔮 Future of Bioinformatics: Share your view on what you want and don't want the future of AI-augmented bioinformatics to be like.
  • 🧬 Model Feedback: Share examples of how the professional practice model helped you build or improve something, like a new workshop or a course curriculum.

docs.title: How to contribute to the practice model
docs.text:
If you would like to propose edits to the practice model directly, follow these steps:

  1. Fork the repo (or just click edit in your browser).
  2. Make your changes in a new branch.
  3. Submit a Pull Request to the main branch.
  4. Wait for review. One of our maintainers will check your changes for accuracy and adherence to our professional practice model.
  5. Get credited. Once merged, we use the all-contributors bot to add your profile to our contributors page!

Please note that this project is released with a Code of Conduct. By participating, you agree to abide by its terms.

docs.title: Contributors
docs.text: This project is made possible by the time and expertise of the following community members. Thank you!

docs.title: Want to be listed here?
docs.text: See our contributing guide to learn how you can add your name to this list!

docs.title: Delegation
docs.text:
According to Anthropic's 4D Framework for AI fluency Delegation means "Setting goals and deciding whether, when, and how to engage with AI".

In Bioinformatics, delegation requires a deep understanding of the biological context, breaking complex problems into small independent tasks, determining for which tasks it's appropriate to use generative AI, choosing the most appropriate AI-based or non-AI-based tool for each task, and knowing how to implement agentic workflows following best practices.

Table of Contents

Description in Bioinformatics applies to the following professional practice areas:

  • Delegation
    • Framing the biological problem and formulating the solution roadmap
    • Deconstructing the solution roadmap into actionable tasks
    • Orchestrating autonomous AI workflows to execute tasks ar scale

docs.title: Framing the biological problem and formulating the solution roadmap
docs.text:
• Hypothesis generation

    • Leverage domain expertise to formulate novel biological hypotheses and questions in research areas where existing data or AI training models are limited

  • Innovative problem sovling

    • Devise alternative strategies or creative workarounds when standard computational approaches or AI models fail to solve a complex biological problem.

  • Workflow architecture selection

    • Select the most effective combination of traditional bioinformatics tools and AI models to solve a specific biological problem.

  • Cross disciplinary integration

    • Incorporate multidisciplinary context and biological constraints to define the boundaries of a given computational solution.

docs.title: Deconstructing the solution roadmap into actionable tasks
docs.text:
• Modular task decomposition

    • Break down a complex bioinformatics problem into clear, individual tasks that can be performed independently.

  • Technical acceptance criteria definition

    • Define the exact expected outputs, data formats, and quality benchmarks for each task before starting execution.

  • Awareness and Task-AI Alignment (ACTIVE Framework)

    • Critically assessing the complexity of a task to determine if AI integration is appropriate or if it risks bypassing essential learning.

  • Data Science Domain Knowlegde (ISCB Competency Framework)

    • ISCB D3: Use data science methods suitable for the size and complexity of the data.
    • ISCB F3: Make appropriate use of bioinformatics tools and resources.

docs.title: Orchestrating autonomous AI workflows to execute tasks ar scale
docs.text:
• Multi-agent architecture design

    • Architect end-to-end automated workflows that leverage chained AI agents to execute complex, multi-stage bioinformatics analyses.

  • Human-in-the-loop checkpoints

    • Engineer programmatic checkpoints and strategic pause points within automated workflows to surface high-stakes AI decisions for human validation.

  • Computational resource management

    • Manage computational resources, API limits, and token budgets to keep automated workflows running efficiently.

  • ISCB Computer Science Domain

    • ISCB H3: Make appropriate and efficient use of scripting and programming languages.

  • ACTIVE: Transparent integration

    • Maintaining "human-in-the-loop" governance for all computational conclusions

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
According to Anthropic's 4D Framework for AI fluency Description means "Effectively describing goals to prompt useful AI behaviors and outputs.".

In Bioinformatics, description requires effectively communicating goals, context, and assumptions to AI agents in the form of instructions to drive the expected behaviors and make outputs reproducible and interpretable.

Table of Contents

Description in Bioinformatics applies to the following professional practice areas:

  • Description
    • Translating biological knowledge into precise AI instructions
    • Structuring raw biological data for AI model context
    • Documenting and tracking AI interactions and software parameters for reproducibility

docs.title: Translating biological knowledge into precise AI instructions
docs.text:
• Bioscience domain knowledge (ISCB Competency Framework)

    • ISCB A3: Work at depth in at least one technical area aligned with the life sciences.

  • Domain knowledge translation

    • Translate deep domain-specific biological mechanisms, technical terminology, and experimental variables into precise computational instructions for AI execution.

  • High-context prompting

    • Write structured, unambiguous prompts using advanced techniques to guide AI analysis.

  • Adaptive instruction

    • Redirect AI systems dynamically when they drift from the expected analytical path or task boundaries.

docs.title: Structuring raw biological data for AI model context
docs.text:
• Metadata standardization

    • Apply highly descriptive system instructions that translate experimental designs and dataset annotations into clear parameters for AI ingestion.

  • Bioscience domain knowledge (ISCB Competency Framework)

    • ISCB B3: Prepare life science data for computational analysis.

docs.title: Documenting and tracking AI interactions and software parameters for reproducibility
docs.text:
• Provenance logging

    • Record every prompt text, system configuration, model version, and seed value used to achieve a computational result.

  • Automated documentation

    • Generate exhaustive documentation that describes the functions, parameters, and expected outputs of AI-assisted code.

  • Version control

    • Track changes to scripts, prompts, and configurations regularly using version control systems like GitHub.

docs.title: Diligence
docs.text:
According to Anthropic's 4D Framework for AI fluency Diligence means "Taking responsibility for what we do with AI and how we use it.".

In Bioinformatics, diligence is assuming accountability and responsibility for the impact of agentic AI solutions. This is not only about adhering to international standards for ethical and safe AI use, but also about maintaining the cognitive abilities to remain critical of AI-generated outputs and complete tasks manually when AI agents fail.

Table of Contents

Diligence in Bioinformatics applies to the following professional practice areas:

  • Diligence
    • Ensuring pipeline governance and regulatory compliance
    • Securing digital asset longevity and sustainability
    • Countering mental dependency while preserving technical mastery
    • Nurturing a supportive and responsible scientific community
  • Guiding institutional strategy and cross-sector diplomacy
    • Elevating workforce capacity and educational legacy

docs.title: Ensuring pipeline governance and regulatory compliance
docs.text:
• Professional Conduct (ISCB Competency Framework)

    • ISCB J3: Comply with professional, ethical, legal, and social standards.

  • AI regulatory alignment

    • Monitor rapidly shifting regional and global AI legal frameworks to dynamically align pipeline deployments with local jurisdiction mandates.

  • Ethical evaluation

    • Identifying algorithmic bias and ensuring that AI-driven decisions are calibrated, safe, and legally compliant.
    • Formulate institutional/team risk guardrails to prevent algorithmic discrimination, dual-use biological research hazards, or intellectual property infringements.

docs.title: Securing digital asset longevity and sustainability
docs.text:
• Data Science Domain Knowledge (ISCB Competency Framework)

    • ISCB E3: Manage own and others' data according to community standards (FAIR principles).

  • Computer Science Domain Knowledge (ISCB Competency Framework)

    • ISCB I3: Construct and maintain bioinformatics computing infrastructure.
    • ISCB G3: Contribute to the design of user-centric bioinformatics tools.

docs.title: Countering mental dependency while preserving technical mastery
docs.text:
• Iterative skill development (ACTIVE Framework)

    • Countering "cognitive offloading"—the habit of delegating reasoning to tools—by intentionally practicing the "cognitive musculature" of manual coding and statistical reasoning .

  • Professional Conduct (ISCB Competency Framework)

    • ISCB M3: Engage in continuing professional development

docs.title: Nurturing a supportive and responsible scientific community
docs.text:
• Professional Conduct (ISCB Competency Framework)

    • ISCB L3: Work effectively in teams to accomplish common goals.
    • ISCB K3: Communicate meaningfully with a range of audiences.

  • Bioscience Domain Knowledge (ISCB Competency Framework)

    • ISCB C3: Have a positive impact on scientific discovery through bioinformatics.

docs.title: Guiding institutional strategy and cross-sector diplomacy
docs.text:
• Visionary stewardship

    • Design long-term technical roadmaps, governance models, and sustainability plans to secure the permanent operational longevity of bioinformatics initiatives.

  • Scientific diplomacy

    • Navigate complex negotiations and communicate impact across academia, industry, and government sectors to align competing interests and influence decision makers.

docs.title: Elevating workforce capacity and educational legacy
docs.text:
• Generational mentorship

    • Develop early-career talent by actively mentoring them to leverage generative AI systems appropriately, critically, and responsibly.

  • Pedagogical dissemination

    • Design open-access learning resources that leverage cloud and AI technologies to democratize access to scientific discoveries and dismantle structural educational inequities.

docs.title: Discernment
docs.text:
According to Anthropic's 4D Framework for AI fluency Discernment means "Accurately assessing the usefulness of AI outputs and behaviors.".

In Bioinformatics, discernment means using deep biological intuition to identify mistakes in AI-augmented bioinformatic analyses, and utilizing community standards to assess performance and confidence. It's also about calibrating one's belief in AI outputs against reference knowledge to ensure biological plausibility and statistical validity.

Table of Contents

Discernment in Bioinformatics applies to the following professional practice areas:

  • Discernment
    • Evaluating the biological plausibility of AI outputs.
    • Benchmarking AI results against standard baselines
    • Detecting computational artifacts and hallucinations

docs.title: Evaluating the biological plausibility of AI outputs.
docs.text:
• Biological integrity verification

    • Verify the biological plausibility of AI-generated results against established mechanistic principles in your specific life science domain, and trusted reference sources or databases.
    • Critical Verification Protocols (ACTIVE Framework): Implementing structured validation procedures to audit AI-generated code or biological hypotheses.

  • Statistical auditing

    • Assess the statistical assumptions, significance estimates, and error measurements of AI-generated results to verify mathematical rigor.

  • Biological interpretation and contextualization

    • Synthesize AI-generated results with established knowledge to extract meaningful biological insights and draw valid conclusions.

docs.title: Benchmarking AI results against standard baselines
docs.text:
• Algorithmic bias detection

    • Screen AI outputs for population skews, data imbalances, or training data biases across diverse experimental groups.

  • Automated evaluation design

    • Build programmatic evaluation pipelines that automatically calculate error rates and compare AI outputs against gold-standard biological datasets or software tools.

  • Verification Confidence Calibration (ACTIVE Framework)

    • Closing the "proof-belief gap" by aligning one's belief in an AI solution's correctness with objective accuracy outcomes.

docs.title: Detecting computational artifacts and hallucinations
docs.text:
• Hallucination detection

    • Identify fabricated data, non-existent literature citations, or flawed logical loops within AI text and code outputs.

  • Collaborative code review

    • Engage in structured peer code reviews, pull requests, and issue tracking on GitHub to systematically verify the accuracy and logic of AI-generated code in an iterative manner throughout the project's lifecycle.

  • Code auditing and debugging

    • Audit AI-generated scripts line-by-line to expose syntax errors, silent logical bugs, or hardcoded variables before running them.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
This professional practice model is based on the following competency frameworks:

Anthropic. (n.d.). Anthropic's 4D framework for AI fluency. Retrieved May 2026, from https://www-cdn.anthropic.com/334975cdec18f744b4fa511dc8518bd8d119d29d.pdf?ref=hackshackers.com

Brooksbank, C., Brazas, M. D., Mulder, N., Schwartz, R., Ras, V., Morgan, S. L., Lloret Llinares, M., Carvajal López, P., Larcombe, L., Ghouila, A., Hancocks, T., Satagopam, V., De Las Rivas, J., Mazandu, G., & Gaeta, B. (2024). The ISCB competency framework v. 3: A revised and extended standard for bioinformatics education and training. Bioinformatics Advances, 4(1), Article vbae166.

EMBL-EBI. (n.d.). EMBL-EBI Competency Hub: ISCB 3.0 framework. Retrieved May 2026, from https://competency.ebi.ac.uk/framework/iscb/3.0

A pedagogical framework and its first classroom implementation in response to automation bias, cognitive debt, and the verification paradox. (n.d.). EdArXiv. Retrieved May 2026, from https://osf.io/preprints/edarxiv/vhwbn_v1
